data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
08:30 AM Durable Goods Orders 
 
Durable goods orders are new orders placed with domestic manufacturers for factory hard goods. The report also contains information on shipments, unfilled orders and inventories.
 
After soaring in May, aircraft orders are down in June, which is expected to leave total durable goods orders down 11.0 percent. Ex-aircraft is seen up 0.1 percent and core capital goods up 0.2 percent, not a bad showing.
1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count 
 
The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

data.title: United Rentals raises free cash flow outlook to $2.6B for 2025 amid robust specialty and ancillary growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: United Rentals (URI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Matthew J. Flannery, CEO, reported that United Rentals delivered "solid second quarter results, which reflected a continuation of the momentum we reported last quarter" and noted confidence in updated guidance, supported by both management and customer sentiment. Flannery highlighted that total rental revenue grew by 4.5% year-over-year to $3.9 billion, with rental revenue rising 6.2% to $3.4 billion—both described as second quarter records. He also detailed robust performance in both industrial and construction end markets and noted that "Specialty rental revenue grew 14% year-over-year, while opening 21 cold starts in the second quarter." Flannery underscored the utility vertical's growth, stating, "the utility vertical is now north of 10% of our revenue versus 4% fewer than 10 years ago," and mentioned a recent five-year agreement with a major utility customer.

  • Flannery explained the company's technology investments, citing enhancements in telematics and fleet management as key drivers of customer productivity and repeat business.

  • William Edward Grace, CFO, stated, "As Matt just shared, 2025 continues to progress as expected, with second quarter records across total revenue, rental revenue and EBITDA. Looking ahead, our updated guidance reflects the demand we see across our end markets supported by our differentiated strategy and strong execution." Grace highlighted "rental revenue increased $200 million year-over-year or 6.2% to a second quarter record of over $3.4 billion," and noted the ongoing impact of ancillary and re-rent revenue, which grew by roughly 10% year-on-year, adding $59 million of revenue.

Outlook

  • Grace announced updated guidance with the midpoint for total revenue raised by $100 million, narrowing the range to $15.8 billion to $16.1 billion, and full year growth of roughly 4% at midpoint. The company expects adjusted EBITDA between $7.3 billion to $7.45 billion, with the midpoint up $50 million from previous guidance. Free cash flow guidance increased by $400 million to a range of $2.4 billion to $2.6 billion, with Grace attributing this to "the benefits of recently enacted tax reform, which reinstated full expensing of CapEx and thus, will reduce our cash taxes." Used sales guidance is unchanged at $1.45 billion on approximately $2.8 billion of OEC sold.

  • Flannery reiterated that CapEx expectations remain unchanged and emphasized the go-to-market strategy and one-stop-shop value proposition as core elements supporting ongoing growth.

Financial Results

  • United Rentals reported adjusted EBITDA of $1.81 billion for Q2 2025, a second quarter record. Adjusted EPS for the quarter was $10.47. Rental gross profit contributed $86 million, and ancillary and re-rent revenue added $59 million. Used equipment sales delivered $317 million in proceeds at an adjusted margin of 48.3% and a 53% recovery rate.

  • The company generated free cash flow of $1.2 billion year-to-date and expects to return nearly $2.4 billion to shareholders in 2025. Net leverage was 1.8x at the end of June, with total liquidity of $3 billion.

Q&A

  • David Michael Raso, Evercore ISI, inquired about price/cost dynamics and margin impacts from ancillary mix. Flannery responded, "The one that we're pointing to as outsized, for lack for a better word, is a delivery, which is also in its ancillary... as that becomes less prevalent that we won't have that same level of impact and drag of ancillary in the back half of the year." Grace added, "We do think we're managing price costs very effectively as we continue to manage both through those cost dynamics I talked about and the investments we continue to make."

  • Michael J. Feniger, BofA Securities, questioned the free cash flow baseline going forward. Grace replied, "You can kind of tack it on and assume that, that number has gone up on the order of $400 million."

  • Angel Castillo, Morgan Stanley, asked about customer sentiment following tax reform. Grace indicated, "We've seen our customer confidence feedback stay high levels and probably get fractionally better versus where we would have been in April."

  • Clay Williams, Goldman Sachs (for Jerry Revich), asked about drivers of fleet productivity acceleration. Flannery emphasized, "We feel really good about the fleet productivity... we expected to drive positive fleet productivity this year, which, in its basic form, means we're going to have rent revenue growth greater than fleet growth, and that's happening."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised pointed questions about margin dilution from ancillary growth, sustainability of free cash flow, and visibility into 2026, with a neutral to slightly positive tone due to management's clear explanations and upward guidance revisions.

  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone, repeatedly stating the year is "playing out as expected" and expressing optimism in ongoing tailwinds and the robustness of the business model, as seen in statements like "We feel really good about the environment that we're in right now."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from reaffirming guidance to raising outlook, while analysts maintained a focus on margin dynamics and the implications of tax changes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the midpoint of revenue, EBITDA, and free cash flow guidance raised, compared to reiterated guidance in Q1.

  • Specialty rental growth moderated to 14% year-over-year from 22% in Q1, while ancillary revenue growth also slowed but remained strong. Adjusted EBITDA margin saw a 100 basis point compression, compared to 60 basis points in Q1.

  • Analysts continued to probe margin headwinds from ancillary mix and delivery costs, but the focus shifted to the implications of tax reform and elevated free cash flow outlook in Q2.

  • Management's overall sentiment was more confident, citing improved customer confidence and successful execution of strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Margin compression was attributed to the outgrowth of lower-margin ancillary revenue and higher delivery costs, particularly from the Yak acquisition.

  • The company continues to operate in an inflationary environment, with ongoing investments in specialty cold starts and technology impacting margins.

  • Analysts flagged concerns about the sustainability of ancillary growth, ability to price for value-added services, and visibility into 2026 amid shifting project dynamics and macroeconomic factors.

Final Takeaway

United Rentals delivered a strong second quarter with record rental revenue and EBITDA, raising its full-year free cash flow outlook to $2.6 billion and narrowing guidance ranges. Management highlighted disciplined execution, robust customer sentiment, and strategic investments in specialty and technology as key growth drivers, while acknowledging ongoing margin headwinds from ancillary growth and inflation. The company remains focused on capital allocation, shareholder returns, and leveraging its position as a value-added partner across core verticals.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Edwards Lifesciences raises 2025 sales growth outlook to 9-10% amid robust TAVR and TMTT momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Edwards Lifesciences (EW) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Bernard Zovighian highlighted a sharpened focus strategy aligned with anticipated regulatory approvals and expansion across key product lines, noting, "These areas represent large and growing opportunities, and we are just getting started." He stated the company achieved double-digit sales growth in Q2, with total sales of $1.53 billion growing 10.6%. He announced an increase in full-year 2025 sales growth guidance to 9% to 10% and adjusted EPS guidance to the high end of $2.40 to $2.50. Zovighian emphasized, "Our focus on structural heart has positioned the company for agile execution of our strategy and provides the foundation for sustainable growth."

  • Zovighian reported global TAVR sales of $1.1 billion, up 7.8% year-over-year, and referenced catalyst events such as SAPIEN free platform approvals for asymptomatic indication in both the U.S. and Europe. He announced that Larry Wood, long-time leader of the TAVR team, would depart in early September and be succeeded by Dan Lippis.

  • On the TMTT business, Zovighian reported second quarter sales of $133 million, up 57%. He stated, "PASCAL and EVOQUE, were both significant contributors to growth as they continue to scale." He also confirmed the completion of enrollment in a 1,000-patient European post approval study for PASCAL, and the ongoing launch and evidence-building for EVOQUE and SAPIEN M3.

  • Surgical product group Q2 sales reached $267 million, rising 6.8%. Zovighian underscored positive global procedure growth and highlighted new data for RESILIA durability as well as CE Mark approval for KONECT.

  • CFO Scott Ullem stated, "Our double-digit sales growth drove adjusted earnings per share of $0.67. Our GAAP EPS for the quarter was $0.57, which included a onetime charge related to our portfolio of external investments." Ullem further clarified, "For the second quarter, our adjusted gross profit margin was 77.6%, in line with our expectations compared to 80% in the same period last year."

Outlook

  • The company raised its full-year 2025 sales growth guidance to 9% to 10%, targeting sales of $5.9 billion to $6.1 billion. Ullem stated, "We are increasing our underlying growth rate guidance for TAVR to 6% to 7%, driven by strong performance and our sales guidance range for TAVR to $4.3 billion to $4.5 billion."

  • Full-year adjusted earnings per share guidance is now at the high end of the original $2.40 to $2.50 range. For Q3, projected sales are $1.46 billion to $1.54 billion and adjusted EPS of $0.54 to $0.60.

  • Management reiterated expectations for mid-single-digit growth in the Surgical product group and $530 million to $550 million in full-year TMTT sales.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted operating profit margin was 28.2%. Selling, general, and administrative expenses were $502 million or 32.8% of sales. Research and development expense was $276 million, or 18% of sales. Ullem reported, "We continue to expect our full year 2025 adjusted gross profit margin to be within our original guidance range of 78% and 79%."

  • The company maintained a strong cash position with $3 billion in cash and cash equivalents as of June 30, and $1 billion remaining under its share repurchase authorization.

Q&A

  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: Asked about the drivers of better-than-expected U.S. TAVR performance and whether asymptomatic indications contributed. Zovighian responded, "The quarter really came better than expected for TAVR...we created a catalyst in the TAVR space. And the good news about that, it is just starting." Larry Wood added, "I don't think we're seeing a lot of asymptomatic patients come in. I think...what we're seeing is just a renewed attention on the management of patients with severe aortic stenosis."

  • Marcus followed up on non-U.S. trends, particularly Europe and Japan. Dan Lippis noted, "The rollout of our S3 platform is progressing really nicely...the recent asymptomatic indication...is going to be a real game changer for the longer term."

  • Travis Steed, Bank of America: Asked about EPS guidance and longer-term leverage. Ullem stated, "We ended up coming in above the top end of our guidance range, which was not expected...we've got some headwinds...but overall, we're feeling good about the trends in earnings per share this year."

  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Queried about the timing and implications of reopening the NCD. Wood responded, "We think the time to open the NCD is now...there are a number of changes that need to be made...making sure that asymptomatic gets covered."

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: Questioned real-world EVOQUE outcomes. Daveen Chopra explained, "What we've seen in the real world is actually results that are similar or better than TRISCEND II...it's a continued data set after data set showing similar real-world outcomes."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a positive tone, frequently congratulating management on the quarter and raising questions about sustainability and future catalysts. Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking, emphasizing execution and robust momentum. Zovighian stated, "I am super proud of the team...We have a very bold strategy altogether."

  • During Q&A, management provided detailed, constructive responses and reiterated confidence, while also acknowledging ongoing uncertainties such as tariffs and acquisition impacts. The previous quarter featured more cautious optimism, while the current quarter reflected increased confidence.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised FY2025 sales growth guidance from 8%-10% to 9%-10%, and TAVR guidance from 5%-7% to 6%-7%.

  • Adjusted EPS guidance was tightened to the high end of the initial range. Q2 saw accelerated TAVR and TMTT growth, with TMTT sales up 57% versus 60% in Q1 on a lower base.

  • Management’s confidence improved, highlighting execution of strategic initiatives and successful product launches, while analysts’ tone shifted from cautious optimism to more positive engagement.

  • Leadership transition in TAVR, with Larry Wood departing and Dan Lippis assuming the role, was a notable management change.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs and the pending JenaValve acquisition remain potential headwinds. Ullem noted, "We continue to expect lower second half operating margin levels compared to the first half, reflecting expenses associated with the planned acquisition of JenaValve."

  • Market access for new products, particularly SAPIEN M3 in the U.S. and EVOQUE expansion, was discussed as an ongoing challenge.

  • Analyst questions centered on regulatory timing for NCD revisions, reimbursement, and real-world product performance, as well as the ability to sustain high growth rates.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized the strength of the company’s focused strategy and breakthrough innovation, highlighting robust sales growth, upgraded guidance, and expanding global leadership in structural heart therapies. With multiple product launches, a balanced portfolio, and a strong financial position, Edwards Lifesciences expressed confidence in delivering value to patients and shareholders through 2025 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Knowles signals accelerated organic growth in 2025 with Q3 EPS guidance of $0.29–$0.33 amid robust order activity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Knowles Corporation (KN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Jeffrey Niew, President and CEO, highlighted the company's resilience in the face of evolving tariffs, stating Knowles is "well positioned as it relates to the direct and indirect impacts of tariffs," with total exposure under 5% of revenue and 3% of cost of goods sold. Niew added, "In Q2, we have had success in passing these additional costs on to our customers, and our expectation is to continue to do so without loss of business."
  • Niew reported, "In Q2, we had a strong quarter, delivering revenue of $146 million, up 8% year-over-year, and cash from operations of $36 million, both exceeding the high end of our guided range. EPS of $0.24 was above the midpoint of the guided range, up 20% year-over-year."
  • MedTech & Specialty Audio revenue reached $67 million, up 13% sequentially and 10% year-over-year, with strength in both Specialty Audio and Hearing Health markets. Precision Devices segment revenue was $79 million, up 8% sequentially and 6% year-over-year. Niew emphasized, "The bookings trends was broad-based across most of our end markets and the distribution demand has returned as we believe inventory levels have normalized."
  • Niew highlighted new initiatives, noting that the recently announced inductor line "has the potential to expand our TAM and drive future growth," while the specialty film production line expansion is expected to increase organic growth rates beyond historical levels.
  • $30 million in share repurchases were funded by strong cash generation. Niew stated, "We believe our strong cash generation will allow us to pursue synergistic acquisitions and buy back shares while continuing to keep our debt at very manageable levels."
  • John Anderson, Senior VP and CFO, reported, "We reported second quarter revenues of $146 million, up 8% from the year ago period and above the high end of our guidance range. EPS was $0.24 in the quarter, up $0.04 or 20% from the year ago period and above the midpoint of our guidance range. Cash generated by operating activities was $36 million, exceeding the high end of our guidance range."

Outlook

  • Anderson provided Q3 2025 guidance: revenues between $144 million and $154 million, R&D expenses of $8 million to $10 million, SG&A expenses of $25 million to $27 million, adjusted EBIT margin of 22% to 24%, interest expense at $2 million, and an effective tax rate of 13% to 17%. "We're projecting EPS to be within a range of $0.29 to $0.33 per share."
  • Cash generated by operating activities is projected between $20 million and $30 million, with capital spending expected at $11 million for Q3 and full year capital spending at approximately 5% of revenues.
  • Anderson stated, "With the strong backlog and increased order activity, we expect to continue to sustain both sequential and year-over-year revenue and earnings growth for the remainder of 2025."

Financial Results

  • Q2 revenue reached $146 million, up 8% year-over-year, and cash from operations totaled $36 million.
  • MedTech & Specialty Audio revenue was $67 million, up 10% year-over-year; segment gross margins at 50.6%, down 280 basis points year-over-year, but up 200 basis points sequentially. Precision Devices revenue was $79 million, up 6% year-over-year, with segment gross margins at 38.7%, up 150 basis points from Q2 2024.
  • R&D expenses were $9 million, slightly up from the previous year, and SG&A expenses reached $28 million, up $2 million year-over-year. Interest expense was $3 million, down $2 million from the prior year.
  • The company repurchased 1.9 million shares at a cost of $30 million and ended the quarter with $103 million in cash and $190 million in debt. Net leverage ratio was 0.7x with total liquidity over $350 million.

Q&A

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna: "So the guide was nicely ahead of what we were thinking and perhaps what you guys were thinking earlier in the year. If you could speak to maybe the delta, what is providing that upside? And then also any update on demand for the thin film opportunity as well?" Niew replied, "This is the third successive quarter within the PD segment where we've had a book-to-bill over 1. I would note our book-to-bill in Q2 for PD was above 1.15. So we have the very strong bookings quarter again... we're seeing quite a bit of demand on the PD side."
  • Rolland: "Did you have any early thoughts on how we should be thinking about Q4?" Niew responded, "We are expecting year-over-year growth again in Q4, and we expect sequential growth again in Q4."
  • Rolland: "Just gross margin expansion. Like with these higher volumes, what should we expect from higher utilizations moving forward here?" Anderson answered, "We delivered gross margins of 44.3% in Q2. We expect sequential to be at least up 100 basis points or more in Q3 and kind of similar levels in Q4."
  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities: "You mentioned the film expansion and inductor lines. I was hoping you could just expand on that a little bit..." Niew explained, "The growth in the specialty film line is actually going to be other customers, mainly medtech, defense and industrial shorter term... specialty film is going to really start delivering significant growth in the back half of this year as well as into 2026. The inductor line is going to take a little longer."
  • Labick: "Can you talk just a little bit about the M&A market?... How does it look now?" Niew replied, "Pipeline is good... Q1 into Q2, the market kind of froze up a little bit on doing M&A. It seems like it's starting to reopen again."
  • Anderson commented, "We have a lot of optionality. I would say, with our low leverage and our strong cash flow generation, we continue to have a lot of optionality with respect to capital allocation."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was positive, with multiple congratulations on strong results and curiosity about upside drivers, new product lines, and M&A opportunities.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, with Niew stating, "I am excited by the momentum and strength the business demonstrated and the growth opportunities that we have in front of us, both in the near and longer term."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from cautious optimism to clear confidence, supported by robust order activity, expanded product lines, and successful tariff impact management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance and actual results surpassed the previous quarter’s upper range, with revenue up from $132 million in Q1 to $146 million in Q2 and EPS increasing from $0.18 to $0.24.
  • Management’s confidence in sequential and year-over-year growth was stronger, with explicit commentary on accelerating organic growth and expanded product offerings.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused on upside surprises, gross margin expansion, and product pipeline as opposed to Q1’s focus on tariff impacts and customer demand stability.
  • The tone of management’s responses in Q2 was more assertive about future growth and the effectiveness of their business model.

Risks and Concerns

  • Niew acknowledged ongoing monitoring of the tariff situation, noting it remains "fluid," but reiterated that Knowles' direct and indirect exposure is limited and costs are being successfully passed to customers.
  • Gross margin pressures in MedTech & Specialty Audio were attributed to "unfavorable product mix and higher factory costs," though sequential improvement was recorded.
  • The company is closely watching industrial market demand for signs of recession impact but reported no current negative effects.

Final Takeaway

Management highlighted a strong Q2 performance with revenue and EPS growth exceeding guidance, supported by robust bookings and normalized inventory levels across end-markets. The business is positioned for accelerated organic growth into 2025, enabled by new product introductions and expansion initiatives. With ongoing share repurchases, disciplined capital allocation, and confidence in sustained sequential and year-over-year growth, Knowles signals continued momentum for the remainder of 2025 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Boston Beer raises gross margin guidance to 46%-47.3% amid shipment declines and brand investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Boston Beer Company (SAM) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Founder and Chairman C. James Koch stated that the company is operating in "a challenging and unpredictable macroeconomic environment" with "economic uncertainty and household budget tightening, along with pressure on Hispanic drinkers" negatively impacting demand in the beer industry. Koch emphasized "long-term growth opportunities in Beyond Beer," which now represents over 85% of Boston Beer's volume.
  • Koch highlighted, "Despite a weaker-than-expected volume environment, we delivered strong margin expansion and EPS growth in the quarter. This was driven by continued progress on our productivity initiatives." He noted operating cash flow of over $125 million in the first half and $111 million in cash returns to shareholders year-to-date.
  • CEO Michael Spillane reported, "Our strategy to nurture all our core brands, pursue a fewer things better approach to innovation and transform our supply chain is gaining traction." He noted growing depletions on 4 of 7 brands and a record high in customer service levels with gross margin approaching 50%. Spillane cited Sun Cruiser as "one of the top volume gainers in RTD spirits so far this year" and stated that the brand tripled its points of distribution this summer.
  • Spillane explained that Twisted Tea held share in the F&B category with dollar sales declining 4% last quarter, but brands like Twisted Tea Light and Twisted Tea Extreme are growing shelf space and velocities. Spillane described Sun Cruiser as "gross margin accretive" and said it has "quickly grown to a 4 share of the RTD spirits category."
  • CFO Diego Reynoso reported, "Depletions in the second quarter decreased 5% and shipments decreased 0.8% compared to the second quarter of last year, primarily driven by declines in the Truly Hard Seltzer and Sam Adams brands that were only partially offset by growth in the company's Sun Cruiser and Dogfish Head brands."
  • Reynoso added, "Revenue for the quarter increased 1.5% due to increased pricing and favorable product mix, partially offset by lower volumes. Our second quarter gross margin of 49.8% increased 380 basis points year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Reynoso stated, "We now expect our volume to be down high single digits to down low single digits for the year. We're providing a wide volume range as there are still many weeks of the summer selling season ahead of us and the timing of any improvements in the overall beer industry remain uncertain."
  • The company raised full-year gross margin guidance to 46% to 47.3%, up from the previous range of 45% to 47%. EPS guidance is $8 to $10.50 before tariffs and $6.72 to $9.54 including tariffs.
  • Reynoso expects price increases of 1% to 2% and noted, "We continue to expect increases in advertising, promotional and selling expenses to range from $30 million to $50 million."

Financial Results

  • Reynoso reported EPS of $5.45 per diluted share for Q2, an increase of 24.1% compared to the prior year. Operating cash flow for the first half exceeded $125 million.
  • Gross margin was 49.8% for the quarter, up 380 basis points year-over-year, attributed to "improved brewery efficiencies, procurement savings, price increases and product mix, which were partially offset by increased inflationary and tariff costs."
  • Advertising, promotional and selling expenses increased by $15.5 million, or 10.7% year-over-year, driven by increased brand investment in media.
  • The company reduced capital expenditure guidance by $20 million to a new range of $70 million to $90 million, prioritizing support for productivity programs.
  • Cash balance at quarter-end was $212.4 million with an unused $150 million credit line.

Q&A

  • Filippo Falorni, Citi: Asked about the slow summer start in beer and the relative performance of Twisted Tea versus Sun Cruiser. Koch responded, "The industry is off 4% or 5%, us a little bit less. So we have gained volume and dollar share this year." He added that "about 20% of the drop in Twisted Tea is attributable to the vodka tea category," and expects some lost Twisted Tea volume to recover as conditions normalize.
  • Peter Grom, UBS: Questioned the more negative implied volume guidance. Spillane answered, "The depletion started a lot softer for the industry in the second quarter than we expected. And therefore, that rebalance is going to be a little bit harder than we saw at the same depletion forecast."
  • Nadine Sarwat, Bernstein: Inquired about the lowered tariff cost estimate and Sun Cruiser's long-term growth. Spillane stated, "We've learned a lot of lessons here. We've done a lot of things right... we're building this on a strong foundation... So we're confident. We know it's a long run." Reynoso clarified that the tariff impact is "net of mitigation within our suppliers."
  • Eric Serotta, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the sustainability of high gross margins. Spillane said, "We're very happy with the performance... that's allowed us to offset some of the tariffs that we've seen so far."
  • Bonnie Herzog, Goldman Sachs: Questioned shipment and depletion guidance range and Twisted Tea outlook. Reynoso cited high volatility in the market as the reason for the widened range and emphasized prudence given uncertainty.
  • William Joseph Kirk, ROTH Capital: Asked about potential for negative earnings in the second half and D9 (THC beverage) plans. Spillane said negative earnings in Q4 is possible if market trends worsen, and Koch described the hemp-based THC market as too volatile for immediate investment.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern about softness in beer demand, the volatility of key brands (Twisted Tea, Truly), and the wide range of guidance, with repeated questions about underlying drivers and the realism of forecasts. The tone was neutral to slightly negative, pressing for clarity on volume and margin sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, highlighting margin gains and brand innovation, but became more defensive and cautious in Q&A, frequently citing "volatility" and "uncertainty" in the market. Koch and Spillane stressed, "We're controlling what we can control" and "we're confident" in long-term prospects while acknowledging short-term challenges.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautiously optimistic to more cautious and defensive, especially around volume and brand-specific headwinds. Analysts were more persistent in probing the sustainability of margins, guidance rationale, and brand growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company lowered its volume outlook from "down low single digits to up low single digits" in Q1 to "down high single digits to down low single digits" for the full year in Q2.
  • Gross margin guidance was raised from 45%-47% to 46%-47.3%, reflecting strong progress on productivity initiatives despite tariff headwinds.
  • EPS guidance was reiterated before tariffs but refined post-tariff, with the estimated cost impact of tariffs lowered from $20-$30 million to $15-$20 million.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from innovation and early Sun Cruiser rollout to more pointed questions on guidance range, shipment/depletion balance, and the risk of negative earnings, reflecting a more skeptical outlook on near-term recovery.
  • Management's confidence in Twisted Tea growth moderated, shifting from expectations of "low single digit growth" in Q1 to acknowledging possible declines in Q2 and discussing the need to address pricing and shelf space dynamics.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "economic uncertainty and household budget tightening," "pressure on Hispanic drinkers," poor weather, and increased competition in FMB and RTD categories as key risks.
  • Tariff costs were highlighted as a headwind, with management estimating a $15-$20 million cost for 2025 after mitigation.
  • Management plans to continue productivity initiatives, brand investment, and careful inventory control as mitigation strategies.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the wide guidance range, negative shipment trends, and the potential for negative earnings in the back half of the year.

Final Takeaway

Boston Beer management emphasized that despite industry headwinds and an increasingly volatile environment, the company is committed to driving long-term value through brand investment, productivity initiatives, and innovation. While shipment and depletion trends have softened, margin expansion and strong cash flow offer flexibility to invest and return capital to shareholders. Management remains focused on executing its strategy and adapting to evolving market dynamics, while closely monitoring risks such as consumer demand, tariffs, and competitive pressures throughout the remainder of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intel outlines $18B 2025 CapEx and signals strategic foundry shift amid cost cuts and AI focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intel Corporation (INTC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Lip-Bu Tan stated that "we had a solid Q2 with revenue above the high end of our guidance," emphasizing strong demand and team execution. He noted headline profitability was impacted by one-time items and impairments but highlighted underlying operating performance improvements.
  • Lip-Bu Tan detailed four major initiatives: organization/culture, foundry strategy, x86 franchise, and AI. He reported a systematic organizational review, targeting a year-end employee count of 75,000 with a 50% reduction in management layers, and a return-to-office mandate for September to streamline operations and improve agility.
  • On foundry strategy, Tan said, "transforming this unique asset into a robust foundry business requires us to take a systematic approach and act from the position of strength," confirming the cancellation of manufacturing projects in Germany and Poland, consolidation of assembly and test operations in Costa Rica, and a slowdown in Ohio construction to align spending with demand.
  • For product development, Tan emphasized, "our top priority is delivering our first Panther Lake SKU by year-end," with additional SKUs planned for the first half of 2026. He also noted ongoing leadership changes in the data center business to be announced next quarter.
  • On AI, Tan admitted past shortcomings, stating, "we approached AI with a traditional silicon and training-centric mindset without a cohesive silicon systems software stack and strategy," and outlined plans to build a full-stack AI solution, focusing on inference and agentic AI.
  • CFO David Zinsner reported, "second quarter revenue was $12.9 billion, coming in above the high end of our guidance range," and explained the impact of one-time impairment and restructuring costs on gross margin and EPS. He stated, "Q2 gross margin was 29.7% and EPS of minus $0.10. Excluding these charges, our second quarter non-GAAP gross margin would have been 37.5% and non-GAAP EPS would have been $0.10, both results ahead of our Q2 guidance."

Outlook

  • The company forecasts Q3 revenue between $12.6 billion and $13.6 billion, with a midpoint gross margin of approximately 36% and breakeven non-GAAP EPS, citing a cautious approach due to recent above-expectation revenue and continued tariff uncertainty.
  • Operating expense targets remain at $17 billion for 2025 and $16 billion for 2026. CapEx guidance for 2025 is $18 billion gross and $8 billion to $11 billion net, with lower CapEx expected in 2026.
  • Tan reiterated, "I am very confident in our ability to hit our operating expenses targets for 2025 and 2026, respectively."

Financial Results

  • Q2 revenue was $12.9 billion, above guidance, with Intel Products revenue at $11.8 billion, and All Other revenue at $1.1 billion. Operating profit for Intel Products was $2.7 billion, representing 23% of revenue.
  • Intel Foundry delivered Q2 revenue of $4.4 billion and an operating loss of $3.2 billion, impacted by $800 million in impairment charges.
  • Q2 operating cash flow was $2.1 billion, gross CapEx was $4.5 billion, net CapEx was $3.1 billion, and adjusted free cash flow was negative $1.1 billion. Cash and short-term investments totaled $21.2 billion.
  • The company raised approximately $900 million from a Mobileye offering and expects to complete the Altera transaction in Q3.

Q&A

  • Ross Clark Seymore, Deutsche Bank: Asked how quickly the x86 and foundry trust can be rebuilt. Tan responded that progress is being made on 18A, with confidence in launching Panther Lake by year-end and the importance of building trust through reliability and yield.
  • Seymore followed up on gross margins. Zinsner identified Lunar Lake ramp and early-stage Panther Lake costs as near-term headwinds, but expected Panther Lake to provide a margin tailwind next year as volumes increase.
  • Timothy Michael Arcuri, UBS: Queried foundry strategy and 14A development. Tan explained that lessons from 18A are informing 14A, and early customer engagement is building confidence, but CapEx will only be allocated when performance and volume commitments are validated.
  • Arcuri asked about CapEx reductions. Zinsner said about half of the $18 billion CapEx is sustaining, and next year’s CapEx will be less than $18 billion but above $9 billion.
  • Joseph Lawrence Moore, Morgan Stanley: Asked about Intel 7 supply constraints. Zinsner attributed it to strong Raptor Lake demand, with ongoing capacity investments for Intel 4.3 products.
  • Moore asked about construction in progress. Zinsner detailed reductions in assets under construction and plans to maintain optionality for fab space.
  • Benjamin Alexander Reitzes, Melius: Asked about server trends and share. Zinsner acknowledged ongoing competitive gaps but noted improvements with Granite Rapids and upcoming products.
  • Reitzes queried AI strategy. Tan outlined a focus on inference, agentic AI, and adding more software talent for a full-stack approach.
  • William Stein, Truist: Inquired about AI positioning and possible ASIC strategy. Tan described a full-stack solution leveraging x86, openness to new architectures, and collaboration with system companies for purpose-built AI platforms.
  • Stein asked about write-downs. Zinsner clarified these were mainly equipment impairments, not inventory.
  • Stacy Aaron Rasgon, Bernstein: Questioned 18A/14A timing and foundry sustainability. Tan confirmed commitment to foundry, with cautious CapEx deployment, while Zinsner noted 18A’s long-term utility for internal and eventual external customers.
  • Vivek Arya, BofA: Asked about server ASPs and ARM competition. Tan said Intel retains 55% market share and is working to close performance gaps, especially with hyperscalers and high-end enterprise.
  • Arya followed up on gross margins. Zinsner said 40%-60% revenue fall-through is a rule of thumb for next year.
  • Aaron Christopher Rakers, Wells Fargo: Sought clarity on server roadmap and Diamond Rapids timing. Tan indicated a 2026 target, with ongoing roadmap reviews and new leadership planned.
  • Rakers asked about SCIP headwinds. Zinsner said prior guidance remains appropriate, updating as needed in future periods.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed pressing concerns over foundry strategy, margin headwinds, competitive positioning, and clarity on CapEx. Questions were direct, with some skepticism around execution and the pace of strategic shifts.
  • Management adopted a confident tone in prepared remarks. During Q&A, responses were measured and acknowledged challenges, with Tan stating, “I have a lot more confidence... the culture is changing and you guys are really focused on the yield rather than just the performance.” Zinsner provided clear justifications for margin and CapEx plans, often referencing ongoing reviews and flexibility.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a similarly optimistic but pragmatic tone, while analysts remained focused on execution and competitive threats.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance was more cautious, reflecting three consecutive quarters of revenue above expectations but continued tariff and economic uncertainty.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward financial discipline, with explicit cancellation and consolidation of manufacturing projects not previously disclosed.
  • Management’s cost control and organizational streamlining efforts advanced from discussion to execution, with completed headcount reductions and management layer cuts.
  • Analyst focus shifted from general transformation and cost structure to more granular questions on foundry viability, CapEx flexibility, and AI product roadmaps.
  • Key financial metrics saw modest sequential changes, with Intel Products revenue and operating profit holding steady, while Intel Foundry reported a deeper loss due to impairments.
  • Management’s confidence in meeting OpEx and CapEx targets increased, with more concrete plans and milestones cited.

Risks and Concerns

  • One-time impairment and restructuring charges weighed on profitability, with $800 million in tool impairments and $1.9 billion in severance and related costs.
  • Capacity investments outpacing demand were flagged, leading to project cancellations and consolidation.
  • Intel 7 node supply constraints remain a challenge, as does competitive pressure in server CPUs and ARM’s encroachment.
  • Tariff uncertainty and regulatory risks continue to impact visibility on demand and cost structure.
  • Success of foundry strategy hinges on achieving volume commitments and execution on new nodes (18A/14A), with CapEx tied to external and internal customer adoption.

Final Takeaway

Intel’s management emphasized a decisive shift to financial discipline and operational streamlining in Q2 2025, marked by project cancellations, organizational restructuring, and a renewed focus on core product and foundry execution. The company is steering CapEx to $18 billion with the flexibility to lower it in 2026, while targeting $17 billion in OpEx for 2025. Progress on 18A and Panther Lake remains central, with management confident that improved execution and a disciplined approach to customer commitments will position Intel to rebuild trust, drive returns, and regain competitiveness in key markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Coursera raises 2025 revenue outlook to $746M amid accelerated consumer growth and AI-driven platform enhancements
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Coursera (COUR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gregory M. Hart reported "Coursera delivered a strong second quarter. We are executing at a renewed and rapid pace, delivering revenue of $187 million and increasing our growth to 10% year-over-year." Hart announced, "We are raising our expectations for full year revenue and adjusted EBITDA. We now expect to deliver $738 million to $746 million of revenue, raising the midpoint of our range by $17 million. We are also increasing our annual adjusted EBITDA margin target to 8%, delivering 200 basis points of year-over-year improvement."
  • Hart highlighted the addition of 7.5 million new registered learners, the company's "largest number of quarterly new additions since 2020," bringing the cumulative base to 183 million. The enterprise customer base grew to 1,686, a 12% annual increase. He cited the appointment of Patrick Supanc as Chief Product Officer and Grant Parsamyan as Chief Data Officer, stating they "hit the ground running, working alongside our teams to shape our next chapter of innovation."
  • The CEO noted Coursera's generative AI catalog now includes more than 925 courses, with over 10 million enrollments in generative AI courses so far in 2025. Coursera was named to the Time 100 Most Influential Companies list for expanding access to generative AI skills, and signed a new collaboration with the University of Cambridge.
  • Hart introduced product innovations including Coursera Coach, an AI-powered tutor, stating "learners using Coach are 10% more likely to pass a quiz on their first attempt." He emphasized expansion of AI Translations and AI Dubbing, now enabling more than 120,000 learners to complete over 400,000 learning hours in multiple languages, especially in Spanish-speaking markets.
  • CFO Kenneth R. Hahn stated, "We delivered another solid quarter, generating total revenue of $187 million, up 10% from a year ago, driven by growth in both our consumer and enterprise segments." Hahn added, "Gross profit was $105 million, up 13% year-over-year with a 56% gross margin, up 180 basis points from 54% in the prior year period."

Outlook

  • For Q3 2025, Hahn stated, "we expect revenue to be in the range of $188 million to $192 million, representing growth of 7% to 9% year-over-year weighted towards our Consumer segment. For adjusted EBITDA, we're expecting a range of $10 million to $14 million."
  • For the full year, Coursera is "raising our expectations for both revenue and adjusted EBITDA" to a range of $738 million to $746 million, with an 8% adjusted EBITDA margin target, an increase of 200 basis points over the prior year.
  • Hahn explained that improvement "is concentrated in our Consumer segment by nature of its more responsive revenue model," while enterprise assumptions "have not changed as we continue to monitor and assess the current corporate spend environment."

Financial Results

  • Coursera reported $123 million in Consumer segment revenue, up 10% year-over-year, and $64 million in Enterprise segment revenue, also up 10% year-over-year.
  • Consumer segment gross profit reached $75 million with a 61% margin, and Enterprise segment gross profit was $45 million with a 70% margin.
  • Free cash flow was $29 million, and Coursera ended Q2 with $775 million in unrestricted cash and no debt.
  • Net income stood at $19 million, and adjusted EBITDA was $18 million, or 9.6% of revenue.

Q&A

  • Stephen Hardy Sheldon, William Blair: Asked about guidance deceleration in Q4 and AI education priorities among big tech. CFO Hahn responded, "The macro trends in enterprise, we don't think we're getting any better visibility, which is something affecting broadly the market. So it's the Consumer segment." CEO Hart added, "We are very well positioned with all the right foundational assets... We have a very healthy and improving fundamentals to the business."
  • Taylor Anne McGinnis, UBS: Inquired about consumer growth sustainability and upcoming product/go-to-market changes. CFO Hahn said, "We expect Q3 to be similar to Q2 from a growth standpoint for consumer. And then to slow a little bit in Q4 with typical seasonality as well as some pullback on Degrees." Hart noted growth acceleration was "broad-based" and emphasized the early stage of product rollouts.
  • Bryan Michael Smilek, JPMorgan: Asked about enterprise vertical trends and AI monetization. CFO Hahn highlighted, "C4C has been a particularly bright spot for us... government business, we lapped some contracts this last year... Coursera for Business hasn't had as much improvement." CEO Hart discussed the expansion of the Gen AI catalog and the use of AI tools to improve engagement and optimize content.
  • Ryan Michael MacDonald, Needham: Focused on gross margin sustainability. Hahn replied, "We expect those trends to continue. It will vary always a bit quarter-to-quarter depending on the mix... but by and large, we've improved the operating model around consumer."
  • Further analyst questions explored new hires, product and data strategies, regional growth drivers, and the impact of Coursera produced content investments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts consistently pressed on sustainability of consumer growth, enterprise macro visibility, and monetization of AI-driven engagement, with a tone that was neutral to slightly positive as they acknowledged improvements and sought clarity on future growth levers.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, especially in prepared remarks. CEO Hart repeatedly framed the company as "positioned with all the right foundational assets," while CFO Hahn emphasized operating discipline and outlook improvements. During Q&A, management was measured but optimistic, using language such as "we are very well positioned" and "we expect those trends to continue."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive on acceleration and innovation, while analysts maintained a focus on growth durability and enterprise risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Coursera raised its full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance this quarter, now targeting $738 million to $746 million in revenue and an 8% EBITDA margin, compared to $720 million to $730 million and a 7% target previously.
  • Consumer segment growth and new learner additions accelerated, with 7.5 million new learners this quarter versus 7 million in Q1. Total registered learners rose to 183 million from 175 million.
  • Management's confidence and pace of innovation increased, with new senior hires and expanded investments in AI-powered learning experiences.
  • Analysts' focus shifted more towards the sustainability of consumer momentum and enterprise segment visibility, while management provided more detailed product and go-to-market updates.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified macroeconomic uncertainty as a primary risk for the enterprise segment, with CFO Hahn stating, "the current corporate spend environment... could remain challenged for any macro uncertainty."
  • There is acknowledgment of seasonality and a pullback in Degrees revenue in Q4, potentially weighing on overall growth.
  • Analysts pointed to the challenge of sustaining improved conversion, engagement, and retention metrics over time, and pressed management for more data-driven progress indicators and clarity on product rollout impacts.

Final Takeaway

Coursera's second quarter of 2025 marked a period of accelerated growth, raised financial guidance, and substantial product innovation, particularly in AI-powered learning and global content expansion. Management underscored the company's strong financial position, the success of its consumer business, and the strategic investments in product and data leadership. While macroeconomic uncertainty persists in enterprise, Coursera remains focused on leveraging its scale, content, and technology to drive further value for learners, customers, and shareholders through the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Associated Banc-Corp outlines 5%–6% loan growth for 2025 as margin hits 3.04% amid ongoing asset remix
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Associated Banc-Corp (ASB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Andrew John Harmening highlighted that "we're leveraging a best-in-class value proposition to grow and deepen our customer base organically" and reported the "best organic checking household growth we've seen since we began tracking nearly a decade ago." He noted that commercial and industrial (C&I) loan balances have grown by over $700 million through the first half of 2025, stating the company is "well on our way to exceeding the $1.2 billion target we set for the year." Net interest income reached $300 million, described as "the strongest number we've seen in our company's history." Harmening further emphasized that "we added another 9 basis points to CET1 capital in Q2 and have added 19 basis points of CET1 so far this year, following the completion of our balance sheet repositioning."
  • CFO Derek S. Meyer stated, "In the second quarter, our net interest margin of 3.04% was driven by a 5 basis point increase in earning asset yields and a 4 basis point decrease in interest-bearing liability costs." Meyer also commented, "Based on our latest expectations for balance sheet growth and mix, deposit betas and Fed action, we now expect to drive net interest income growth of between 14% and 15% in 2025."
  • Chief Credit Officer Patrick E. Ahern explained, "Our ACLL increased by $5 million in Q2 to finish the quarter at $412 million... The increase in commercial largely stemmed from a combination of loan growth, plus normal movement within risk rating categories."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed expectations for "total bank loan growth of 5% to 6% for the year" and continues to expect "core deposit growth by 4% to 5% in 2025."
  • Meyer said, "We now expect to drive net interest income growth of between 14% and 15% in 2025. This forecast assumes 3 Fed rate cuts in 2025."
  • Harmening expressed confidence in the company's ability to meet its deposit growth targets, citing "recent pipeline and household trends."

Financial Results

  • For Q2 2025, Associated Banc-Corp reported earnings of $0.65 per share.
  • Net interest income hit $300 million, increasing by $14 million from the prior quarter and by $43 million from the same period a year ago.
  • Noninterest income reached $67 million, up 14% over Q1, which management attributed to the absence of the prior quarter’s $7 million mortgage sales loss.
  • Total noninterest expense was $209 million, down slightly from Q1. The efficiency ratio fell below 56%, which management flagged as "the lowest level we've seen since early 2023."
  • Return on tangible common equity was 12.96% in Q2, a 62 basis point improvement from Q1.
  • C&I loan balances expanded by $356 million in Q2, with year-to-date growth exceeding $700 million.
  • The net charge-off ratio increased by 5 basis points to 0.17%.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about the path to achieving core customer deposit growth guidance for 2025. Harmening responded, "A big piece of it is seasonal... What's different going into the second half of this year is we have a commercial pipeline report on deposits... That pipeline has gone up $500 million in the last 12 months."
  • Tamayo followed up on deposit guide increases. Meyer explained, "That's just wholesale funding and brokered CDs. So that's a GAAP number, but what we're focused in is the customer number that Andy was referring to."
  • Tamayo requested an update on the office CRE portfolio. Ahern said, "Office continues to evolve, probably in a better spot than it was a couple of years ago... we're down considerably in what we would deem kind of the more stressed office credits in the portfolio."
  • Robert Scott Siefers, Piper Sandler: Queried margin outlook. Meyer answered, "The piece that we have that has the most predictable impact on our margin strength is the asset side... grinding up the asset yields in a sustainable way, even if rates come down a few clicks."
  • Siefers asked about deposit pricing strategy. Harmening stated, "The discipline we have around pricing is significant... we're hovering around 84% or 85% retention and getting pricing that's been good and helped to become accretive."
  • Terence James McEvoy, Stephens: Asked about updated M&A thoughts. Harmening replied, "If something came to us, it would have to be a really good fit. Strategically, it has to be a good fit financially."
  • McEvoy inquired about the ACL increase for C&I. Harmening responded, "It's a growth story there... you'll see when you have growth, you'll see that ACL rate go up."
  • Christopher Thomas O'Connell, KBW: Asked about positive operating leverage into 2026. Harmening confirmed, "That is the goal going to every single year... it sure looks like we're going to be able to continue on with the positive operating leverage throughout this year and into 2026."
  • O'Connell asked about the hiring pipeline and investment plans. Harmening said, "We've completed our hiring... there is room to add in some key markets should that come available."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of deposit growth, the margin outlook, and credit risk, displaying a neutral to slightly positive tone, with questions probing for risks and upside potential.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, especially in prepared remarks, using phrases such as "we remain well positioned to play offense in the back half of this year" and "we see additional opportunity ahead."
  • During Q&A, management's tone remained disciplined and factual while defending guidance and strategic choices, mirroring the confidence of the previous quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for loan and deposit growth remains consistent, but management raised the net interest income growth forecast from 12%-13% in Q1 to 14%-15% in Q2.
  • The margin improved to 3.04% from 2.97% in Q1, and net interest income rose by $14 million sequentially.
  • Noninterest income rebounded due to the absence of the previous quarter's mortgage sale loss.
  • Analysts' tone was similarly focused on growth sustainability and credit, but management sounded more confident in the scalability of recent investments and operating leverage.
  • Strategic focus remains on asset remix, deposit growth, and disciplined expense management, with expanded hiring and market entry efforts now largely complete.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, especially related to tariffs and interest rates, as a challenge, but noted clients are "planning for tariff changes for some time" and expressed comfort with client preparedness.
  • Ahern reported heightened monitoring of CRE office exposure but described the portfolio as "in a better spot than it was a couple of years ago."
  • Credit metrics such as delinquencies and criticized loans are being closely watched, with management maintaining "a proactive and conservative approach relative to credit risk ratings."

Final Takeaway

Associated Banc-Corp’s management underscored the company’s record net interest income, robust C&I loan pipeline, and ongoing strategic asset remix as key drivers of improved profitability and capital strength. With loan and deposit growth targets reaffirmed and operating leverage trends intact, leadership emphasized confidence in achieving full-year goals, supported by disciplined expense management and ongoing credit vigilance amid a shifting macroeconomic landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Moelis & Company outlines aggressive PCA expansion and CEO transition as revenues hit record $365M in Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Moelis & Company (MC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Kenneth David Moelis, CEO, highlighted that "our revenues in the second quarter and first half of the year reflect the investments we've made over the last few years, our globally integrated platform and our team's relentless focus on executing for our clients." He noted entry into the second half of the year with "a significantly improved transaction environment" and a pipeline "currently sits near record levels."
  • The CEO emphasized the growing momentum in capital markets, stating that the team "achieved record revenues in the first half of the year" and outlined plans to "aggressively scale into a market leader" in private capital advisory (PCA) after hiring three senior bankers in the space.
  • Moelis also announced a CEO transition: "With the firm in such a strong position financially, strategically and culturally, the Board and I determined this was the right time to elevate our next generation of leadership." Navid Mahmoodzadegan will become CEO, while Moelis will move to Executive Chairman.
  • CFO Christopher Callesano stated, "We reported $365 million of revenues in the second quarter, an increase of 38% versus the prior year period and our highest second quarter revenues on record. Our first-half revenues of $672 million were up 39% from the prior year period." He added, "Our second-quarter compensation expense ratio was accrued at 69%, consistent with last quarter. Our second quarter non-compensation expense ratio was 14.4%."
  • Mahmoodzadegan, incoming CEO, said, "As CEO, I will continue to focus on the principles that have been key to our success over the past 18 years, an intense focus on clients, investing in our firm's talent, fostering innovation through the adoption of new ideas and technologies and, of course, driving returns for our shareholders."

Outlook

  • Management stated, "We continue to anticipate the full-year growth of non-compensation expense to be approximately 15% compared with the prior year."
  • The team signaled strong business momentum, with Moelis saying, "I'm confident in our team's ability to execute for our clients" and that the firm enters "the back half of the year with momentum across the business."
  • The pipeline is "currently near record levels," and investor risk appetite is growing, supporting expectations for continued strong activity.

Financial Results

  • Reported $365 million of revenues in Q2, up 38% from the prior year period, marking "our highest second quarter revenues on record" (CFO Callesano).
  • First-half revenues reached $672 million, an increase of 39% year-over-year.
  • Compensation expense ratio for Q2 stood at 69%, unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Non-compensation expense ratio for Q2 was 14.4%.
  • The Board declared a regular quarterly dividend of $0.65 per share.
  • Cash and liquid investments of $475 million with no debt were reported.

Q&A

  • Devin Patrick Ryan, Citizens JMP: Asked about the pace of sponsor reengagement and sector trends. Moelis described a recovery: "From about April 2 to 5 or 6 weeks later, middle of May, there were a lot of people sitting on their hands. ... I'd say we're kind of back as of now very close to the level of enthusiasm that was prior to April 2, March 31."
  • Ryan also inquired about the addressable market for PCA. Moelis responded, "We see it as a -- in a leadership position, it's a couple of hundred million dollars or more, more than a couple of hundred million, we think, in the leadership positions. ... we're going to be aggressive on it."
  • Kenneth Brooks Worthington, JPMorgan: Asked about Navid's focus as new CEO. Mahmoodzadegan emphasized continuity: "the strategy that we've been deploying for the last couple of years ... is the strategy that we've all been developing together that I expect to continue to carry on."
  • Worthington followed up on hiring pace. Moelis stated, "We're going to accelerate into things like PCA. So you're going to see us be very aggressive in the private capital advisory group."
  • James Edwin Yaro, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the possibility of a "big bang" in M&A and IPO activity post-Labor Day. Moelis said, "I am predicting that if there's not an external event from here on in, the market is improving almost daily."
  • Brendan James O'Brien, Wolfe Research: Probed on restructuring trends. Moelis answered, "for this year, it's trended flattish to slightly down. My guess is it continues to trend slightly down."
  • Ryan Michael Kenny, Morgan Stanley: Sought clarity on comp ratio formula. Moelis stated, "this year, there's no formula. I think it's going to be driven by the top line."
  • Alexander Scott Bond, KBW: Queried on revenue mix. Moelis answered, "Pretty dead on, very close to that, yes. It might be a percentage point or higher on M&A, but not much."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts generally expressed optimism mixed with probing questions about the pace and sustainability of the business rebound, the PCA buildout, compensation, and the CEO transition. The tone was largely neutral to slightly positive, with congratulatory remarks for the incoming CEO and constructive questions about strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone throughout the prepared remarks and Q&A. Moelis and Mahmoodzadegan repeatedly stressed aggressive growth plans, especially in PCA, and confidence in the current leadership team. Statements such as "I'm confident in our team's ability to execute for our clients" reflected high confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management demonstrated increased confidence, with less focus on macro uncertainty and more on growth execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 featured record revenues and a significant improvement in the transaction environment, contrasting with Q1's concerns about post-April 2 market volatility and pipeline delays.
  • The CEO transition was formally announced in Q2, marking a shift in leadership focus.
  • Strategic emphasis shifted from uncertainty management in Q1 to aggressive PCA expansion and market share capture in Q2, with management stating intentions to "aggressively scale into a market leader" in PCA.
  • Analysts' questions in Q2 centered more on growth execution and less on macro headwinds than in Q1.
  • The compensation expense ratio remained constant at 69% across both quarters.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the potential for external events to disrupt the improving market, with Moelis noting, "if there's not an external event from here on in, the market is improving almost daily."
  • Concerns about restructuring revenue decline were noted, but management positioned this as a function of a strong capital markets environment.
  • The firm plans to continue aggressive hiring in PCA but has not overstaffed, with Moelis stating, "I'm not worried about it getting slower. I'm worried about us getting up to speed."
  • No new debt and a strong balance sheet were highlighted as risk mitigants.

Final Takeaway

Moelis & Company reported record revenues in Q2 2025 and signaled strong momentum going into the second half of the year, supported by aggressive expansion in private capital advisory and a leadership transition. Management expressed confidence in the firm's ability to capitalize on improving market conditions, maintain disciplined cost controls, and pursue strategic hires to drive future growth, positioning the company for continued shareholder returns and sector leadership.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
